Trash cans haven’t always been around for families to dispose of their trash in. So what did they do with their garbage? That depended on where they lived.

Farmers and their families turned food scraps into pig slop. Of course, pigs will eat anything, even dead bodies, so who knows what was fed to them. What wasn’t fed to the pigs was buried in a hole in the ground or burned. So for those who lived on farms, trash disposal wasn’t that much of an issue.

But what about those who lived in the cities, especially those in apartment buildings? They wouldn’t own their own property to dig holes and bury it. Tenants might own pets, but it is doubtful they would have their own pigs. And burning trash inside an apartment probably wouldn’t be a good idea if you wanted to keep your apartment.

What options were left? Residents didn’t want the trash to pile up inside their home, so they simply threw it out the window and onto the street. If there was already too much garbage in the street—it wouldn’t take long for it to accumulate—it could be hauled to a river or some other body of water and dumped. Thinking about the stench all of the trash plus the fact that horses would urinate and defecate on these same streets makes me thankful for trash cans, garbage men, and the system of waste disposal we now have. And it also makes me thankful that everyone doesn’t tr
top_profane.title: Talking Trash

We are all familiar with the visual of the crotchety, old man shouting at cars driving 3 mph over the speed limit in his neighborhood. When we see him we may think to ourselves, "Geeze guy, settle down, you're going to pull something..." before it dawns on us that before long, WE will be that crotchety old man or woman. For me anyway, there is this realization that given how jaded I already am at 35 about all of life's little annoyances that full-on crochetyness is an inevitability...and that it isn't all that far off.

What makes me particularly salty are all the CRAP products out there that CRAP OUT on you before they should. When did it become a bleeping foregone conclusion that 80% of consumer products will turn to worthless CRAP in mere months? $200 for this? CRAP in a heartbeat. $120 for that? Yep, turned to CRAP in lightning speed. Sure, the more you spend the lesser the probability the product will be CRAP but there is no hard and fast rule where "you spend X and you're guaranteed to avoid CRAP." It's a poor commentary on the whole human race in my opinion...but I digress.

Every so often however, in the midst of thousands upon thousands of CRAP products here and CRAP products there, you come across a gem. THIS BLENDER, LADIES AND GENTLEMAN, IS THAT GEM. I think I paid $29 for this thing when I bought it a few months ago. I figured meh...

This item is one of those where you wonder whether it is worth the price, and we are still wondering. Consider that trash cans are available from the local discount store, or even Amazon, for a tenth of the price of this trash can, or less, and the magnitude of this purchase is suddenly realized. Even so, this trash can is definitely the most sophisticated and beautiful trash can we have ever owned.

The best, and worst, part about this trash can is the automatic opening feature. The trash can opens in two ways. First, there is a sensor that detects movement of a hand (and other body parts) near the trash can, which initiates opening of the trash can. Second, this trash also opens with a voice commend. However, sometimes the trash can randomly opens by itself. So, is it some kind of issue with the sensor, or is the voice command detecting sound and interpreting it as the "open can" command? We are still experimenting, but have been unable to figure it out - so far. Regardless, when the can opens somewhat randomly it can be disconcerting.

One cool part of the automatic opening is technology behind the opening. The motor is relatively low torque, but a planetary gear reduction system provides plenty of power to open and close the lid, with minimal sound. Wow. My partner and I struggled over trash duty for years — it *was* our least favorite household chore, until this can came along. Now, as the title states, I actually take the trash out now on a regular basis like a normal person without whining even a little bit.

Yes, the price is a bit high, especially for poor millennials who just want to make their lives a little easier, but if you hate trash duty too, this is definitely worth it (try to get one used if possible!).

I didn’t realize until recently that the garbage can we had for years was the reason we hated it so much.
It was a “fancy” one with a split down the middle to separate trash & recycling, and a motion detector to open and close the lid (it would “bite” if we didn’t throw things away fast enough).

In order to take the trash out of that one, we had to switch off the battery, completely remove the 10lb+ lid as well as the bag holder rims, set the filthy things down on the floor or countertop, and grip the bottom of the can with our feet in order to yank out the bags (which nearly always caught & ripped on the sharp edges of the narrow opening, causing garbage-juice-puddles). top_profane.text:
For years, I had the idea to install two pull out trash cans in my kitchen, one for trash and one for recycling. But my two available cupboard openings were only 9" wide, and whenever I researched this (multiple times, hours on end, over the years), it seemed all the ready-made solutions wanted at LEAST 10" wide and even then they had very small trash cans. It looked to me like it should be possible to have a pull out trash that just barely cleared the sides & top and still was pretty big, but no one made it, or I could not find it.

Finally, I decided this HAD to happen, and I'd either find a product or get it custom made. At that time, my search found this Knape & Vogt 35qt product. It called for 9 5/8", I think. However, though my framed opening was only 9", the interior width of my cabinets are 10". Would it work? I decided to gamble, and bought one of these to try.

Initially, I was shocked how easy this was to install. I thought the rails would be separate, and I'd have to mount them on the sides of the cabinetry, and it would be tricky to align & mount. However, the way both rails are mounted together on a "sled" that mounds to the bottom, it takes literally 2 minutes to install the whole thing! And it seems very stable.

In fact, the two rails-sled contraption is about 8 7/8" wide (maybe 8 15/16"?) at its widest, so it fit right i

Oof--the standard sized trash bag doesn't reach to the bottom, and the whole thing fell in. I thought--that will be okay; once I secure the lid in place it will hold it up. My illusions were shattered after a few more pieces of trash--the lid was not tight enough to hold the bag up. Why on earth would a 13 gallon trash can not hold a 13 gallon trash bag?!
That was annoying, but it wasn't a deal breaker. We just started taking the trash out a little more often, before it was so heavy that it made the bag fall. I found this reçliner very comfortable, easy to assemble and exactly what I've been looking for as a disabled senior on a fixed income.

I think I died and went to heaven when I turned on the heat and vibration features!!

You can choose between 5 minutes and 25 minutes, in 5 minute increments and it kills everything automatically when the timer expires. This is a great safety feature so you don't have to worry about accidentally leaving it on.

One helpful hint; The controller cord is kind of short. Rather than having it come out the bottom or side of the chair, around the side and up over the armrest, run the cord "up into" the chair between the back and seat cushions so that the control comes through the back right corner of the chair. By doing this, you will gain several inches in length. It's easy to do. Remember what you learned in school: The shortest distance between two points is a straight line!!

The electrical connections are pretty much foolproof. They are color coded and self-evident to understand.

The up/down buttons can start/stop in infinite positions throughout the full range by simply taking your finger off the button.

This is great to custom adjust yourself to your ideal position.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I have a septic system and can't install a garbage disposal, so I keep a screen trap in my sink to catch coffee grounds, fruit peels, and the like. Like another reviewer, I had a white plastic coated wire trash bag holder attached to the door under my sink. It was flimsy and hard to clean, but it was so useful to have a trash bin that I could access with one finger. When it finally fell apart, boy did I miss it.... and had dirty spots trailing across the floor from trying to carry wet gunk from the sink to the trash can 6 feet away. I found a crappy one that was even more flimsy and totally unacceptable--the lid would not stay open when lifted, which made it useless for transferring garbage when your hands are full. I looked EVERYWHERE for a replacement like that wire one, but none of the local kitchen stores seem to carry them anymore. Nor could I find any online... until I found this handy and far superior item from SimpleHuman.

This is just great. I love that it comes with a storage bin for the bags, and that the bin catches any drippings from the bags. Many of those shopping bags have little holes in the seams on the bottom, so I always double bag... but sometimes they still leak. No problem, because the bin that holds bags underneath has a tray-style top to catch any drippings. Very smart thinking!

I think that the people who complained about the
top_five_star_obscene.title: Love the SimpleHuman trash system!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This trash can looks great in our guest bathroom. The copper tones go well with any decor, particularly espresso color furnishings. It stands at 9.5 inches tall. The top has a 9.5 inch diameter, large enough to throw trash in from three feet away. It fits a 4 gallon trash bag perfectly. If you will be using the can on hardwood floor, I would place some felt furniture pads, because the bottom of the trash can has no feet. The bottom can scrape on the floor after repeated use. It is made of thick metal. It is very sturdy and doesn’t tip over easily like a plastic trash can. Who want to pick up trash that’s been spilled over? Not me!

This is a very sturdy and stylish trash can that I am fairly sure will last for a long time.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
We have been needing a new kitchen trash can and I selected this one as a replacement for the one we have been using. I have not worked with a motion-activated trash can before this one so I wasn’t sure if there was anything more complex than just waving a hand in front of it and having it pop open. No, that’s as complex as it gets. So far we’re pleased with it.

This is a stainless steel can with a battery-powered lid. The motion sensor is on the top and is obvious (plus, it is marked with a sticker right out of the box). When the batteries are inserted properly (6 AA-batteries), a simple wave of the hand activates the can and the lid pops open. I found that a paper plate is also sufficient, so I don’t think it’s infrared. My concern, then, was that it would open every time someone walked by it, but this has not been the case because of the location and angling of the sensor.

Operation is very smooth and quiet. Unless you’re in the room with the trash can and are listening for it, the lid opening and closing is very nearly silent. When it is triggered, the lid flips up and stays open for about 5 seconds before it closes again. The opening into the trash bag below is wide enough that you likely will never need to touch the can or its contents until you take out the bag. A 13-gallon kitchen bag is sufficient and seems to be ideal.

Our old can had
top_five_star_obscene.title: Quiet Operation and Good Capacity

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Excellent quality, great design, pleasing appearance. The best residential trash can I have seen or owned.

The only quibble is they can't use normal 13 gallon trash bags from the supermarket without modification. The pedal mechanism makes a big hump in the bottom of the can, with two deep holes on either side. A normal trash bag is pulled off the rim when trash is put into the bag and forces it to fall down into these holes. Filling the holes with foam or sand or kitty litter would allow normal bags to be used, but it would be messy. I found that twisting a normal trash bags edge until it wraps tight around the rim, then clipping it with a chip clip allowed normal bags to be used. But since this interferes with the company's raquet of selling bags I imagine this review will not be published for long.

In the end we decided that the trash bags that the company sells, which are designed specifically to fit this trash can, and which are of excellent quality, strength and thickness are a small indulgence that we can accept, so I bought a 20 box of them until I can come up with a better way to use normal bags again.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Excellent can, and excellent (but expensive) bags

top_five_star_obscene.text:
The area I live in gets fruit flies, and if I have a trash can that is open in any way where they can get in, they lay eggs and I see larva crawling around my trash. I almost had a meltdown the first time it happened.

Since then an auto closing trash can with a solid seal is a must. This has that.

The mouth is wide enough that normal trash bags (Glad, Hefty, the usual brands), stretch over it and provide a solid hold. Last trash can I had I had to use rubber bands or something to try to hold the top in place and it never worked quite well enough. Not a problem with this trash can.

I was debating buying the ~$100 versions from simplehuman but I didn't see where the extra money was really going so I decided on this and I am glad I did. Simple trash, not super expensive but better than the cheap alternatives that don't last.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Fits normal trash bags, closes

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I'm the first to literally laugh out loud with derision at the three figure price on this trash can, seriously. That's just beyond insane in our world. However, if you can justify the splurge on a garbage can, then this thing is pretty darn good. I have to admit it's the best garbage can we've ever had.

First of all, it's attractive to look at and it fits right in with our stainless appliances. The curved body gives it a very modern and sleek aesthetic that doesn't scream "Trash here!" The step is light and responsive and the lid rises immediately and silently. It self closes without banging down which I very much appreciate. I also like the fingerprint resistant external material - mainly because it also deters nose prints from our six dogs.

The inside is split between two containers needing two bags. If you're in an area that does separate recycling this is very handy. Just pull out the blue plastic bucket with one type of stuff and everything else is on the other side. For my needs, we just put a bag liner on each side and go about our business. The liners from SimpleHuman are expensive, fortunately, plain old Hefty 13 gallon trash bags and the 13 gallon generic trash bags from Kroger both fit just fine in this can. That means this is a dog resistant, smell resistant, regular bag using, good looking, fully functional, extra capacity trash can
top_five_star_obscene.title: Hands free, noiseless operation. Resists dog nose prints & generic 13 gallon trash bags fit fine

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Here's the 2 things I needed to know and had a hard time finding in reviews:
1) The large side most definitely fits a 13 gallon tall kitchen trash bag. You pull the excess thru the rubber clip (see photo), it stays with no issue until you're ready to take the trash out.
2) You can almost fill an entire 13 gallon bag full of trash in the large side (see photo). If you totally squash your trash down like a maniac, you probably could fill the whole bag. So you do NOT need to purchase weird size trash bags for the large side!!!!!
The smaller side will also hold a 13 gallon bag if you want to put one in there just to keep the can clean. You'll have some excess bag but again- just pull it thru the rubber clip.
The trash can is large- yes of course because it's actually 2 trash cans. But it's not monstrous as some have indicated. I have a small kitchen & it fits better than the 2 separate cans that I was previously using. Really happy with this one - just wish it was available in white when I purchased.
top_five_star_obscene.title: 13 gallon bag-YES

top_five_star_obscene.text: I never thought that I would be writing such a passionate review for a trash can, but this is the greatest trash can ever made. My dog is a petite, but muscular Beagle who has somehow broken into every single 'locking' trash can we've owned. When we found this trash can at Target, we were on our fourth trash can in two years because she had broken the locks on every one. We've had this one now for almost a year and not only has she been unable to break it, it's so durable that she hasn't even damaged the plastic. My four year old nephew also could not open it, so this is the ideal trash can to keep dogs + little hands away from the trash.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Every dog owner needs this trash can
